title: The Purple-Seller's House
subtitle: Psates, Son of Abraamios -- This, in the Theban Province, 592--616 CE

sections.body_html:
The house was falling apart, and the parents were selling it to their own son.

On the twentieth day of Payni, in the tenth year of the emperor Flavius Mauricius – which is to say, June 14, 592 CE – an elderly purple-seller named Psates and his wife Maria set their names to a deed of perpetual sale.1 The buyer was Aurelius Pachymios, identified in the document with a term of precise legal tenderness: ho hemon glyktatos hyios – “our most sweet son.”2 The property being transferred was described, with the unflinching honesty that Egyptian legal scribes brought to real estate, as “a dilapidated house and a cell together with the bare land.”3

The price: three imperial gold nomismata, less one keration.4 Roughly 2.96 solidi – enough, in the economy of a Theban village at the end of the sixth century, to pay a hired craftsman for the better part of a year.5 Not nothing. Not generosity, either.

The sale took place in the village of This, in the Thinite nome of the Theban province.6 Neither Psates nor Maria could write. A local deacon named Psates son of Papnouthios signed on their behalf, and two more clergymen – the deacon Besatas and the priest Athanasios – witnessed the transaction.7 A professional notary named Ioulios drew up the document.8 Everything was done correctly. Everything was done in Greek.

And yet the deed holds a strangeness that no amount of legal formalism can smooth away. Why would parents sell a house to their own son? Why not simply give it? Why charge him nearly three gold pieces for a ruin? The document itself offers no explanation, because the document is not interested in motives. It records a transaction. What happened beneath it – the family negotiation, the worry about the other children, the calculation of what was fair – is left for us to reconstruct from what came after.

What came after was death, division, and a generation of quiet accumulation. The papyri that survive from this family span twenty-four years, six documents, and three emperors.9 They tell the story of an artisan household in Upper Egypt during the decades before the world they knew ceased to exist.

sections.title: I. The Sale
sections.body_html:
Consider the document more closely.

P.Paris 21bis is a deed of perpetual sale – a prasis – written on papyrus and preserved, like so many of the texts that illuminate late antique Egypt, by the dry Upper Egyptian air and the habits of a people who kept their papers.10 The sellers identify themselves with the precision that Roman-era Egyptian documents demanded: “The Aurelii Psates son of Abraamios, whose mother is Tmouthes, purple-seller, and his wife Maria, daughter of Philamon, whose mother is Taniskene.”11 Four generations of family compressed into a single clause: father, mother, maternal grandmother – for both husband and wife. The state wanted to know exactly who you were. It wanted your mother’s name. It wanted your mother’s mother’s name. In a world without photographs or identity numbers, the maternal line was the surest anchor of identity.12

The property they are selling sits on “the lane called that of the heirs of Pachot and the oil-press of the heirs of Pachareabe.”13 No street numbers. No cadastral maps. A house in sixth-century This was located by what stood next to it: the dead man’s oil-press, the dead man’s heirs, the living man’s wall. The boundaries are given with the compass-point formula found in hundreds of Egyptian property documents: south, the oil-press of the heirs of Pachareabe; north, the house of Pebos son of Horos; west, the houses of the heirs of Viktor the priest and of Pebos son of Horos, “into which the door opens”; east, the public lane.14

That phrase – “into which the door opens” – is the kind of detail that brings the dead village to life. The house opened westward, onto the narrow passage between neighboring homes. You stepped out of Psates’s front door and faced the wall of the priest’s house. Not the public lane. The lane was to the east, behind you. The house faced inward, toward the density of village life, not outward toward the road.

And then the great formula, inherited from pharaonic practice and surviving into the Arab period: apo edaphous heōs ouranou – “from the ground to the sky.”15 What was being sold was not just a structure but a column of legal space, from the foundation stones beneath your feet to the open air above your head. Every Egyptian property sale said this. The formula never aged.

The price had been set not by the parties but by a third-party assessor: “upon the assessment of Senouthes the builder from the same village.”16 This was standard practice. A local oikodomos – mason, builder, construction man – walked through the property, looked at the cracking walls and the bare lot, and named a number. Three nomismata less one keration. Senouthes knew the village housing market. He knew what a ruin was worth.

The guarantee clauses are long and emphatic, as guarantee clauses always were. If anyone should challenge Pachymios’s ownership – “whether on account of our name or public obligation or private debt or anything else whatsoever at any time, of necessity” – the sellers or their heirs must repel the challenger at their own expense, or else repay the price in double, plus all improvement costs, plus a penalty of two gold pieces.17 The legal language is formulaic. It is also revealing. The parents were not merely transferring property. They were building a wall of law around their son’s investment. They were protecting him from their own debts, their own obligations, their own creditors – present and future. Whatever they owed the world, the house was now Pachymios’s, and the world could not touch it.

The payment was made “by hand-to-hand payment from the house” – cheiri para cheiras epi tēs oikias.18 The gold changed hands right there, in the ruined house itself. No bank. No intermediary. Pachymios counted out the coins. Psates and Maria received them. The deacon wrote it down.

sections.title: II. The Purple Trade
sections.body_html:
Psates is identified in the document as a porphyropōlēs – a purple-seller.19 So is his son Pachymios. So, we will learn from later documents, is his other son Ioannes.20 The trade ran in the family like a hereditary office. It was, in the world of late antique Egypt, a trade of considerable significance.

The word itself is ancient. In the Acts of the Apostles, the woman called Lydia – the first European convert to Christianity, baptized by Paul in the river outside Philippi – is described as a porphyropōlis, a seller of purple goods, from the city of Thyatira in Asia Minor.21 That was five centuries before Psates. The trade had not changed much. Purple textiles moved through the same commercial networks, from dyer to seller to buyer, in communities scattered across the eastern Mediterranean.

But what was “purple” in sixth-century Egypt?

The legendary Tyrian purple – extracted from the mucus glands of Murex brandaris sea snails, each yielding a fraction of a gram of dye, thousands of snails crushed and boiled for a single bolt of cloth – had been the monopoly of emperors since the fourth century.22 Diocletian’s price edict of 301 CE set the cost of one pound of Tyrian-dyed wool at 50,000 denarii, making it literally worth its weight in gold.23 By the sixth century, true murex purple was reserved for the imperial court: the emperor’s porphyra, the purple chamber in which empresses gave birth, the purple ink in which the emperor signed decrees. To wear unsanctioned Tyrian purple was, technically, treason.

Psates was not selling treason.

The purple of Egyptian village commerce was a different substance entirely. Egyptian dyers had developed plant-based alternatives that produced colors ranging from reddish-violet to deep crimson.24 A Greek papyrus codex from the third or fourth century, now in Leiden, preserves five recipes for making purple dye without sea snails – using alkanet root, pomegranate bark, and lichens in various combinations with lime, vinegar, and urine.25 The results were not identical to murex purple. They were close enough. And they were cheap enough for ordinary people to wear.

A sixth-century inventory from Egypt lists the kinds of goods that circulated in this market: “one garment of purple, one turban-veil with circular design, another tunic-veil of five-dyed purple.”26 Pentabaphos porphyras – five-dyed purple, cloth that had been dipped and re-dipped to deepen the color. Another document, from the seventh century, records someone ordering “a tremissis worth of red-brown, and one of crimson-red, and one of purple-red” – three distinct shades, each priced in fractions of gold coin.27 The market was specific. Customers knew what they wanted.

Psates, then, was a man who dealt in colored cloth. He may have dyed it himself, or he may have bought dyed goods from workshops and sold them to consumers – the term porphyropōlēs means “seller,” not “dyer,” and the distinction matters.28 But as we will see, his son Pachymios later employed hired purple-workers in a workshop, which suggests that the family was involved in production as well as retail.29 They were not merchants in the grand sense – no long-distance traders financing caravans – but artisan-traders embedded in a local economy, making and selling a product that everyone, from the village priest’s wife to the local landowner’s daughter, might want to own.

sections.title: III. From Panopolis to This
sections.body_html:
The deed of sale contains a phrase that opens onto a whole history of migration: Psates and Maria are described as being “from the city of Panopolis, but now for a long time resident in the village of This of the Thinite nome.”30

Panopolis – modern Akhmim, on the east bank of the Nile – was one of the great cities of Upper Egypt.31 Strabo, writing in the first century CE, noted its linen-weaving and stone-cutting industries. By the fifth and sixth centuries it had become arguably the most important textile production center in the Theban province.32 Archaeological excavations of the Akhmim cemeteries have produced a staggering quantity of Coptic-era textiles: tunics with tapestry-woven medallions, hangings depicting Dionysian scenes, shrouds with Christian iconography, all of them testifying to a city where the art of weaving and dyeing had reached extraordinary sophistication.33

This was Psates’s home city. The city where he learned his trade, where his father Abraamios had perhaps learned it before him. A city of weavers and dyers. A city where a purple-seller would have had many competitors.

Perhaps that is why he left.

The document says he had been in This “for a long time” – ek pollou chronou – by 592 CE.34 If he was elderly in 592, and if “a long time” means twenty or thirty years, then the migration may have occurred in the 560s or 570s, during the reign of Justinian or his successor Justin II. We do not know his reasons. The papyrus does not explain the move, because the papyrus is a deed of sale, not a memoir. But the pattern is recognizable: an artisan from a major city relocating to a smaller market town, where competition was thinner and a skilled man could establish himself.

The village of This – Thinis in earlier sources, Tjenu in the pharaonic period – was one of the most ancient settlements in Egypt.35 Egyptian tradition held that Menes, the first pharaoh of a unified Egypt, had come from Thinis, making it the original capital of the country around 3100 BCE.36 By the sixth century CE, that antiquity was irrelevant to daily life. This was a kōmē, a village, administered within the Thinite nome as part of the larger Theban province. Its exact location remains debated by scholars, but it lay near Abydos and modern Girga, in the Nile Valley of Upper Egypt.37

The documents from This reveal a community that was small but not simple. There were oil-pressers operating their presses, builders assessing property values, metalworkers – copper-workers and ironworkers – plying their trades.38 There were vineyards nearby, at a place called Lynochois, producing wine that circulated as a form of currency.39 There were priests and deacons who served as scribes and witnesses, a notarial class whose literacy made the village’s legal life possible.40 And there were migrants from Panopolis who had settled in and stayed.

Psates was one of them. He had come from the city and put down roots in the village. He had bought property – the deed mentions that the house and cell had “come to us by purchase from Viktor son of Psaios and Senouthes son of Phaliout, in accordance with the force of the two old sale documents.”41 Two earlier purchases, now lost, that had given Psates his foothold in This. By 592 he was selling a part of that foothold to his son.

But – and this is crucial – the family had not cut its ties to Panopolis. When the inheritance was divided eight years later, the estate included not only property in This but “two parental house-plots in the city of Panopolis on the lane of the holy place of Apa Kollouthos.”42 They still owned property in the old city. They still called themselves “from Panopolis.” The migration was a branching, not an uprooting.

sections.title: IV. The Parents Die
sections.body_html:
Between the summer of 592 and the summer of 600, both Psates and Maria died.

We do not know the order. We do not know the cause. We do not know whether they died in the same year or years apart, whether illness took them or simple age, whether they were buried in the Coptic cemetery of This or carried back to Panopolis. The document that records the aftermath – P.Paris 20, dated July 14, 600 CE – says only that “their blessed parents Psates and Maria having reached the end of their lives.”43 Teleutēsantōn tōn makarōn autōn goneōn Psatou kai Marias. The word makariōn – blessed, departed – is the standard Christian euphemism. It tells us nothing except that they were dead and that the living called them blessed.

The document that follows their death is remarkable. It is an inheritance division – a dialysis – drawn up among three children: Pachymios, Ioannes, and Tarsene.44 All three are identified as “from this city of Panopolis” – the old identity clinging to the next generation, even though they were living in This.45 Pachymios and Ioannes are both described as purple-sellers.46 Tarsene is described as a widow.47

Three children. Three equal shares. One complicated estate.

The document opens with a Christian invocation that the 592 sale had lacked: “In the name of the Lord and Master Jesus Christ, our God and Saviour.”48 The shift is subtle but significant. The 592 document had begun with the secular imperial formula – “In the reign and consulship of our most divine lord.” The 600 document begins with Christ. Both are dated to the reign of Maurice. The difference may reflect nothing more than the preference of a different notary – one Ioulios in 592, one Dioskoros in 600.49 Or it may reflect a broader cultural drift, the slow deepening of Christian language in the documentary practices of Upper Egypt. By the time of Heraclius, a decade later, the invocation will have expanded further: “In the name of the holy and consubstantial Trinity, Father and Son and Holy Spirit.”50 Theology seeped into the boilerplate.

sections.title: V. Three Children, Three Portions
sections.body_html:
The inheritance document lays out the division with careful precision. “Through a most precise division having been made among all of them of all their paternal and maternal possessions, each has received the one-third share falling to them of all the small estate left by them, movable, immovable, and self-moving, from the expensive to the cheap.”51

That phrase – tēs elachistēs hypostaseōs – “the small estate” – is worth pausing over.52 The family was not wealthy. A few houses in two locations, some craft tools, some workers. Enough to live on, not enough to call rich. The document itself uses the diminutive, the word for littleness. This was a modest inheritance, divided modestly, among three modest heirs.

But modest did not mean simple. The division had to account for something that had already happened: the sale of the dilapidated house to Pachymios in 592, while the parents were still alive. The other two children – Ioannes and Tarsene – now formally acknowledged that they had “no claim against their most beloved other brother Pachoumis” regarding that earlier transaction.53 The house sold in 592 was not part of the inheritance. It belonged to Pachymios free and clear, “in accordance with the force of the sale issued to him and deposited with him.”54

And here, perhaps, we see why the parents had sold the house rather than giving it. A gift to one child could be challenged by the others after the parents’ death. A sale – a genuine commercial transaction, at a price assessed by a builder, paid in gold coins, witnessed by clergy, recorded by a notary – was legally unassailable. Or nearly so. To make it truly unassailable, the siblings added a penalty clause: if either Ioannes or Tarsene should challenge Pachymios’s ownership, they would pay twelve gold nomismata.55 Twelve solidi. A staggering sum for a family of modest means – roughly four times the original purchase price. The penalty was meant to be prohibitive. It was a wall of gold around a sale of ruins.

This was family love expressed in the language of law. The parents had wanted Pachymios to have the house. The siblings agreed. But love, in the documentary culture of late antique Egypt, was not enough. You needed a deed, a price, a builder’s assessment, a notary’s seal, a penalty clause, and three witnesses from the local church.

What remained in common was enumerated with equal precision: “two parental house-plots in the city of Panopolis on the lane of the holy place of Apa Kollouthos, and another parental house in the village of This, and Chariston the copper-worker and another Chariston the ironworker.”56

Two Charistons. The name means “graceful” or “gracious.” Whether these were enslaved persons, bonded laborers, or independent craftsmen who owed the family some contractual obligation is a question the document does not answer directly.57 They are listed alongside the houses, as part of the estate, in a category the document calls “self-moving” – autokinēta – the standard late Roman legal term for livestock and, sometimes, unfree persons.58 The ambiguity is uncomfortable. Were the two Charistons property? Were they people who happened to be shared? The document, characteristically, does not care about our discomfort. It lists them. It divides them by thirds. It moves on.

The Panopolis property sat on the lane of the holy place of Apa Kollouthos – a Coptic saint and martyr whose cult was widespread in Upper Egypt.59 The family’s old neighborhood, back in the city they had left. They still owned houses there. The purple-seller from Panopolis had never fully left Panopolis. His children, born perhaps in This, were still “from Panopolis” in the eyes of the law, still holders of property on a saint’s lane in a city two days upriver.

sections.title: VI. The Workshop
sections.body_html:
Five years after the inheritance division, in January 605 CE, a new document reveals what Pachymios had been building.

SB 1.4503 is an employment contract.60 A man named Dioskoros, son of Harsynios, described as a “hired purple-worker” – misthios porphyropōlēs – contracts with Pachymios “to serve in the craft alongside your other hired workers for a period of two years.”61

Alongside your other hired workers. Pachymios was running a workshop. Not a one-man operation. He had employees – multiple employees – and Dioskoros was joining them. The business that Psates had transplanted from Panopolis to This had grown, under the next generation’s management, into something approaching a small manufactory.

The terms of Dioskoros’s contract are revealing. His wages for the full two years: nineteen artabai of grain.62 Nine in the first year, ten in the second – a modest escalation, perhaps reflecting increasing skill. An artaba of wheat in this period was worth roughly one-third to one-half of a gold nomisma, so Dioskoros’s annual wages amounted to perhaps three to five solidi.63 Approximately the price of the dilapidated house Psates had sold thirteen years earlier. A working man’s pay for a working man’s year.

The wages were in grain, not gold. This is significant. The late antique Egyptian economy ran on two tracks simultaneously: a gold-coin economy for large transactions and a commodity economy for daily life.64 A purple-worker was paid in wheat because wheat was what he ate. The gold nomisma was for buying houses, making loans, paying taxes to Constantinople. Grain was for surviving.

The contract included penalty clauses running in both directions. If Dioskoros left before the two years were up, he would pay Pachymios one gold nomisma in damages.65 If Pachymios dismissed him early, Pachymios would owe full wages for the remainder of the term.66 The symmetry is notable. This was not a master-slave relationship or a patron imposing terms on a helpless dependent. It was a bilateral agreement, with obligations and penalties on both sides, written down, witnessed, and enforceable.

Dioskoros’s mother, Tibellas, was a co-signatory to the contract.67 Perhaps she served as guarantor. Perhaps her presence was required because Dioskoros was young – the document does not give his age. Perhaps she was simply there, as mothers are, watching her son sign himself into service for two years of grinding pigment and dyeing cloth.

The emperor named at the head of the document is no longer Maurice. It is Phocas – “our most serene lord Flavius Phocas, the eternal Augustus and Imperator, year three.”68 In November 602, the centurion Phocas had led a mutiny against Maurice, marched on Constantinople, captured the emperor, and executed him along with his six sons.69 Maurice’s head was displayed on the Augustaion. His body was thrown into the sea. In the village of This, in the Theban province, the notary changed the name at the top of the formula and continued writing contracts.

sections.title: VII. Wine and Debt
sections.body_html:
The following year – October 606 CE – another document reveals a different dimension of Pachymios’s economic life.

SB 1.4505 records a loan.70 A man named Kallinikos, son of Senouthes, “whose mother is Tloullas, hired worker, from the village of This,” borrows from Pachymios one gold nomisma less one-half keration.71 The loan is not to be repaid in gold. Kallinikos will repay in wine: “seventy-five koloba of unadulterated new wine, of seven xestai each, with jars, at the vineyard of Lynochois, at the time of the vintage.”72

Seventy-five koloba of wine. A kolobon held seven xestai – roughly three and a half liters. That is approximately 262 liters of wine for one gold coin.73 The wine would come from the vineyards of Lynochois, a place near This whose name survives only in these loan documents, and it would be delivered “at the time of the vintage, God willing” – theou thelontos.74 If God wills. The harvest is uncertain. The vintage depends on rain, on sun, on the Nile’s flood, on locusts and mildew and the thousand contingencies that stand between a vine and a jar. Kallinikos was borrowing against a crop that did not yet exist.

This was the economy of the Egyptian village. Not the economy of Constantinople, with its banks and its tax collectors and its imperial mint. This was something older: a web of personal obligations, of gold advanced and wine returned, of a purple-seller lending to a laborer and a laborer repaying from the earth. Pachymios was both craftsman and creditor, manufacturer and moneylender. He occupied the position that such men always occupy in agricultural communities: the person with a little surplus cash, who lends it out against the next harvest, and who thereby binds the borrower to him with ties of debt and dependence.

The witness to the wine loan was “Ioannes son of Abraamios, priest from This.”75 The name catches the eye. Psates’s father was named Abraamios. Is this priest Psates’s brother – Pachymios’s uncle? Or merely a man with the same patronymic in a village where names recycled endlessly? The document does not say. The coincidence hangs in the air.

Seven years later, in November 613 CE, the same Kallinikos borrows from Pachymios again.76 This time the loan is smaller: one-third of a gold nomisma less one-quarter keration. The repayment: twenty-five koloba of wine, again at seven xestai each, again at the vintage, again God willing.77 The ratio has held steady – roughly seventy-five koloba per nomisma.78 The relationship has held steady too. The same lender, the same borrower, seven years on. Patron and client, bound by grain and wine and gold.

The emperor named in this document is different again. It is now Heraclius – “our most serene lord Flavius Heraclius, the eternal Augustus and Imperator, year four.”79 In 610, Heraclius had sailed from Carthage with a fleet, overthrown Phocas, and had him dragged before the new emperor’s throne, where Heraclius reportedly asked, “Is this how you have ruled the empire?” and Phocas answered, “Will you rule it better?”80 Phocas was beheaded. His body was burned. In This, the notary changed the name again.

Three emperors in twenty-one years. Two coups. Two executions. And Pachymios the purple-seller went on lending gold and collecting wine.

sections.title: VIII. Emperor Maurice's Egypt
sections.body_html:
What did it mean, in the year 592, to be a subject of the emperor Maurice?

It meant, first and most practically, that you paid taxes. Egypt contributed 2.6 million solidi per year to Constantinople under Justinian, and there is no evidence that the burden lessened under Maurice.81 The gold flowed north – from villagers to tax collectors, from tax collectors to provincial officials, from the grain fleet to the granaries of the capital. It fed the army. It financed the Persian wars. It gilded the churches. It paid for the diplomatic gifts that kept the Avars and the Slavs from flooding the Danube frontier.

Maurice was, by the standards of Byzantine emperors, a competent administrator and a mediocre politician.82 A Cappadocian general who had won his throne through a combination of military reputation and dynastic marriage, he spent his reign fighting the Persians in the east and the Avars in the west, and trying to make the army do more with less. His Strategikon – a military manual attributed to him, though its authorship is debated – is one of the most important treatises on Byzantine warfare.83 He restored the Persian shah Khosrow II to his throne, winning a favorable peace. He reorganized the western provinces into the exarchates of Ravenna and Carthage. He was pious, intelligent, and fatally parsimonious.

It was the parsimony that killed him. When he ordered his Danube army to winter north of the river in 602 – a cost-saving measure that outraged the troops – the soldiers mutinied, elevated the centurion Phocas, and marched on Constantinople.84 Maurice fled by boat, was captured, and was forced to watch the execution of his sons before being beheaded himself. The patriarch Germanos reportedly said that God had punished Maurice for his sins. The people of Constantinople, who had rioted against his austerity, cheered the usurper.

In This, none of this would have mattered much. The village was nearly two thousand kilometers from Constantinople. News arrived late and mattered little. The purple-seller paid his taxes, sold his cloth, bought his houses. The formula at the top of the document changed. The life beneath it did not.

And yet the imperial presence was not entirely abstract. The gold coins in Pachymios’s hand bore the emperor’s face. The dating formula anchored every transaction in the imperial year. The weight standard by which the coins were measured was called “phylloph” or “of the leaves” – phyllōn – a local standard whose precise origin remains obscure, but which tied the village’s commerce to a system of weights and measures that ultimately derived from imperial authority.85 When Psates sold his house for “three imperial simple tested gold nomismata,” the word “imperial” – despotika – was not decorative. It was a statement about whose authority guaranteed the weight and purity of the gold.

sections.title: IX. The Church at the Center
sections.body_html:
Every document in the family archive passes through the hands of churchmen.

In 592: a deacon writes the deed, a deacon witnesses it, a priest witnesses it.86 In 600: the inheritance division is drawn up in the presence of clergy. In 605: the employment contract is witnessed by the village’s literate Christian establishment. In 606 and 613: a priest witnesses the wine loans. In 616: priests witness the final property sale.87

This was not coincidence. In the villages of Upper Egypt at the turn of the seventh century, the church was the literate institution. It was the school, the library, the notarial office. The deacon who signed for Psates and Maria – “I wrote on their behalf at their request, Psates son of Papnouthios, deacon” – was performing a function that was simultaneously spiritual, social, and bureaucratic.88 He could write. They could not. His literacy was a service, offered to his neighbors, guaranteed by his ordination.

The consistency of church involvement across the family’s documents suggests something deeper than convenience. The clergy of This were not merely available scribes. They were the social infrastructure of the village. When you needed to prove that a sale was genuine, you brought a priest. When you needed someone literate to write your contract, you asked a deacon. When you needed a witness whose word would carry weight in any future legal dispute, you turned to the men who stood at the altar.

The family’s own religious geography confirms this centrality. Their property in Panopolis sat on “the lane of the holy place of Apa Kollouthos.”89 Kollouthos was a Coptic martyr-saint, a physician killed during the Diocletianic persecutions, whose cult was especially strong in the Theban region.90 To live on the lane of his shrine was to live in the shadow of Coptic Christianity’s institutional power – the power of saints and their relics, of monasteries and their lands, of a church that was simultaneously the people’s faith and the people’s bureaucracy.

The great White Monastery of Shenoute, one of the most important institutions in Coptic Christianity, stood near Panopolis.91 A will from nearby Antinoopolis, dated 567 CE, names the monastery of “Apa Senouthes in the Panopolite nome” as heir to a man’s entire estate.92 The monastery was a landowner, an employer, a center of learning, a court of moral appeal. Whether Psates and his family had any direct connection to it we cannot say. But they lived within its gravitational field.

The shift in documentary invocations across the family archive traces the theological atmosphere like a barometer. The 592 sale opens with the secular imperial formula. The 600 division opens with Christ. The 613 and 616 documents open with the Trinity.93 The formulas were not chosen by the families but by the notaries – professional scribes who followed the conventions of their time. The conventions were changing. Egypt was becoming, in its documentary language as in its monumental art, more explicitly and elaborately Christian with each passing decade.

sections.title: X. Pachymios Buys Another House
sections.body_html:
On the ninth of Payni, in the sixth year of Heraclius – June 3, 616 CE – Pachymios bought another house.94

He was, by this time, not a young man. If he was in his twenties or thirties when his parents sold him the ruined house in 592, he was now in his fifties or sixties. He had survived his parents, divided their estate with his siblings, built a workshop, hired workers, lent money, collected wine. And he was still buying property.

The sellers this time were two women: Pyras, daughter of Besatas, acting also on behalf of her sister Rose; and Antheria, daughter of Senouthes.95 They were selling “a complete house with all its rights, situated in the same village of This, on the lane called that of the phylake” – the guard-house.96 The property had come to them by inheritance. The price: thirteen gold keratia, roughly half a nomisma.97

The price seems low – far lower than the three nomismata Psates had received in 592 for a ruin. Perhaps this was a smaller house. Perhaps the sellers were in distress and had no leverage. Perhaps the market had shifted. The document does not explain.

What the document does reveal is that Pachymios was consolidating. The eastern boundary of the newly purchased house was described as “the house of Pebos son of Horos and the house of the purchaser Pachymios.”98 He was buying the house next to property he already owned. This was not opportunistic. This was systematic. Over twenty-four years, from the dilapidated ruin of 592 to the guard-house lane property of 616, Pachymios was assembling a compound – a cluster of adjacent buildings, perhaps connected, perhaps separated by shared walls, growing outward lot by lot into a modest commercial and residential complex.

The witnesses to the 616 sale include a priest named Elias son of Besatas and another named Mousaios son of Papnouthios.99 The names echo those from the 592 sale: Besatas son of Elias had been a deacon-witness in 592. Has the son now become a priest? Has the deacon’s son followed his father into holy orders, rising a rank in the process? And Mousaios son of Papnouthios – is he related to Psates son of Papnouthios, the deacon who wrote the 592 deed? The village was small. The names recycled. The families intertwined across the decades, clergy producing clergy, witnesses producing witnesses, the same handful of surnames appearing and reappearing like threads in the weave of the village’s collective life.

The formula by which Pachymios is described in this final document contains a new phrase: he is “from Panopolis, now by the providence of God residing in the same village of This.”100 Nyn de pronoia theou enoikounti. By the providence of God. The earlier documents had said simply “now for a long time resident.” Now it is God’s providence that has placed him here. The notary’s language has become theological. Or perhaps Pachymios himself, aging, had come to see his life’s migration – from Panopolis to This, from the old city to the old village – as something providential. Something intended.

sections.title: XI. What the Record Does Not Say
sections.body_html:
The last document in the family archive is dated 616 CE.

Twenty-six years later, in 642, the Arab general Amr ibn al-As completed the conquest of Egypt.101 The last Byzantine garrison surrendered. The province that had fed Constantinople for three centuries passed to the Rashidun Caliphate. The gold that had flowed north now flowed east.

What happened to the purple-sellers of This?

We do not know. The documentary record falls silent. Pachymios, if he was still alive in 642 – and if he was in his twenties in 592, he would have been in his seventies, which is not impossible – would have seen the Persian occupation of Egypt from 619 to 629, the Byzantine reconquest under Heraclius, and then the Arab armies moving south through the Nile Valley.102 Would he have noticed? The village would have noticed. New rulers meant new taxes, new administrators, new languages on official documents. But the purple trade, the wheat wages, the wine loans, the house-plots on the saint’s lane – these things had survived three emperors and two coups within the lifetime of a single archive. They may have survived the conquest too. Or they may not have.

The Arab-era papyri from Egypt show that many of the old economic patterns persisted well into the eighth century.103 Greek continued to be used in documents for decades after the conquest. The village clergy continued to witness transactions. The artisan trades continued. The vine still grew at Lynochois, presumably, and wine was still pressed, though under masters who prayed toward Mecca.

But the family of Psates son of Abraamios disappears. Whether they died, or moved, or simply stopped generating documents that survived, or generated documents that were lost, we cannot say. The archive ends where the archive ends. The papyrus preserves what the papyrus preserves.

What it preserves is this: a family of purple-sellers from Panopolis who migrated to an ancient village, bought property, practiced their craft, divided an inheritance with fairness and legal precision, employed workers, lent money, collected wine, built a modest compound of houses on adjacent lots, witnessed every transaction through the local church, and lived through the reigns of Maurice and Phocas and Heraclius without, as far as we can tell, the slightest disruption to the daily rhythms of their working lives.

They were not important people. The “small estate” of Psates and Maria was not the kind of fortune that attracted the attention of historians or tax officials or provincial governors. It attracted the attention of a builder named Senouthes, who walked through the ruin and named a price. It attracted the attention of a deacon named Psates, who picked up a pen and signed. It attracted the attention of a notary named Ioulios, who drew up the deed, rolled it, and filed it away.

And it attracted, fourteen centuries later, the attention of anyone who wants to understand how ordinary people lived in the last decades before the ancient world became the medieval one. Not in Constantinople, where emperors were murdered and theology was debated in marble halls. Not in Alexandria, where the patriarch and the prefect maneuvered for power. But in a village in Upper Egypt where a man sold a ruin to his son for three gold coins, and a deacon wrote it down, and the papyrus survived.

Apo edaphous heōs ouranou. From the ground to the sky.

notes.text: P.Paris 21bis (corpus id 25044), dated 14 June 592 CE (Payni 20, Year 10 of Maurice, 11th indiction). The document is a deed of perpetual sale (*prasis*) from the village of This in the Thinite nome, Theban province.

P.Paris 21bis, line addressing the buyer: "to Aurelius Pachymios, our most sweet son, himself also a purple-seller." The Greek *glyktatos* is a superlative form of endearment common in family documents.
P.Paris 21bis: "a dilapidated house and a cell together with the bare land" (*oikian ereipon kai kellin hama tō psilo edapho*).
P.Paris 21bis: "three imperial simple tested gold nomismata less one keration, by the weight-standard of Phylloph." A keration was 1/24 of a nomisma.
For wage comparisons, see SB 1.4503 (corpus id 6311), where a hired purple-worker received 19 artabai of grain over two years, with annual wages equivalent to roughly 3-5 nomismata.
P.Paris 21bis: "in the village of This in the Theban province."

P.Paris 21bis, subscriptions: "I wrote on their behalf at their request, Psates son of Papnouthios, deacon, from This." "I, Besatas son of Elias, deacon, from This, witness the sale." "I, Athanasios son of Paamios, priest, from This, witness the sale."
P.Paris 21bis: "Written through me, Ioulios, notary" (*symboilaiographos*).

The family archive comprises P.Paris 21bis (592 CE), P.Paris 20 (600 CE), SB 1.4503 (605 CE), SB 1.4505 (606 CE), SB 1.4504 (613 CE), and P.Paris 21 (616 CE), spanning the reigns of Maurice, Phocas, and Heraclius.

On the genre of Egyptian property sale documents, the formula *prasis* remained remarkably stable from the Ptolemaic through the Byzantine period, with compass-point boundary descriptions, guarantee clauses, and penalty provisions appearing in documents centuries apart.
P.Paris 21bis, seller identification clause.

The maternal identification system in Egyptian documents served as a safeguard against identity fraud in a culture where paternity could be contested but maternity was observable. The practice has deep pharaonic roots.
P.Paris 21bis, property location: "situated in the same village on the lane called that of the heirs of Pachot and the oil-press of the heirs of Pachareabe."
P.Paris 21bis, boundary description.

P.Paris 21bis: "or whoever the neighbors may be on all sides, from the ground to the sky." The formula *apo edaphous heōs ouranou* appears in Egyptian property documents across centuries. Compare P.Mich. 13.662 (corpus id 1495, 615 CE), which uses "from foundation to the sky above" in a house sale from Aphrodite.
P.Paris 21bis: "upon the assessment of Senouthes the builder from the same village."
P.Paris 21bis, guarantee and penalty clause.
P.Paris 21bis, payment clause.
P.Paris 21bis: Psates identified as *porphyropōlēs*, "purple-seller."
P.Paris 20 (corpus id 5267, 600 CE): "Aurelius Ioannes, himself also a purple-seller, his full brother."
Acts 16:14: "A certain woman named Lydia, a seller of purple (*porphyropōlis*), of the city of Thyatira."

The imperial monopoly on murex purple was codified in the Codex Theodosianus (4th century) and reinforced under Justinian. On the production process, Pliny the Elder (Natural History 9.60-65) describes the extraction and processing of murex dye.
Diocletian's Edict on Maximum Prices (301 CE), Chapter 24, set the price of Tyrian-dyed wool at 50,000 denarii per pound.

On plant-based purple alternatives in Egypt, a Greek papyrus codex from the late 3rd-early 4th century CE (P.Leid. X, now in the Rijksmuseum van Oudheden, Leiden) preserves five recipes for producing purple dye using alkanet root (*Alkanna tinctoria*), pomegranate bark, and lichen-based dyes (orchil/orcein).
P.Leid. X (Stockholm-Leiden papyrus), recipes for purple dye.
P.Coll.Youtie 2.85 (corpus id 26177, 6th century), an inventory of purple garments including "five-dyed purple" (*pentabaphos porphyras*).
P.Louvre 3.234 (corpus id 1953, 7th century), a letter requesting purchases of "red-brown, crimson-red, and purple-red" dyes.

The distinction between *porphyropōlēs* (seller) and *porphyrobapheus* (dyer) or *porphyreus* (purple-worker) matters for understanding the family's economic role. The selling title may indicate a retail function, though the employment of hired workers (SB 1.4503) suggests production as well.
SB 1.4503 (corpus id 6311, 605 CE): Dioskoros contracts "to serve in the craft alongside your other hired workers."
P.Paris 21bis: "from the city of Panopolis, but now for a long time resident in the village of This of the Thinite nome."

On Panopolis/Akhmim as a textile center, see Strabo, Geography 17.1.41; and the extensive corpus of Coptic textiles from the Akhmim cemeteries, excavated in the late 19th century and now dispersed across European and American museum collections.

The textile finds from Akhmim include tunics with Dionysian tapestry-woven medallions (early 6th century), hangings with mythological scenes, and shrouds with Christian iconography, attesting to continuous production from the late Roman through the early Arab period.

On the Akhmim textile finds, see the catalogue of the Textile Centre Akhmim-Panopolis exhibition (Reichert Verlag), which documents material evidence for continuity and change in society, religion, industry, and trade.
P.Paris 21bis: *ek pollou chronou*, "for a long time."
On Thinis/This, see Manetho's *Aegyptiaca* (3rd century BCE), which identifies the first two dynasties as "Thinite." The Egyptian name was Tjenu; the Coptic form is Tin.
The identification of Thinis as the origin of Menes/Narmer and the first capital of unified Egypt (c. 3100 BCE) derives from Manetho and is supported by the proximity of the royal cemetery at Abydos.
The exact site of ancient This remains unidentified. Scholarly consensus places it near modern Girga and the temple complex of Abydos, in the Nile Valley of Upper Egypt.

P.Paris 20 (corpus id 5267) mentions "Chariston the copper-worker (*orichalkeus*) and another Chariston the ironworker (*siderurgos*)." The oil-press of the heirs of Pachareabe is mentioned in P.Paris 21bis. Senouthes the builder appears as property assessor in P.Paris 21bis.
SB 1.4505 (corpus id 6313, 606 CE): wine to be delivered "at the vineyard of Lynochois."

On the role of clergy as scribes and witnesses in Byzantine Egyptian village life, the pattern is consistent across the Thinite nome documents and paralleled in the better-known archives from Aphrodite; see J.G. Keenan, "The Aphrodite Papyri and Village Life in Byzantine Egypt."
P.Paris 21bis: "having come to us by purchase from Viktor son of Psaios and Senouthes son of Phaliout, in accordance with the force of the two old sale documents."
P.Paris 20 (corpus id 5267): "two parental house-plots in the city of Panopolis on the lane of the holy place of Apa Kollouthos."
P.Paris 20: "their blessed parents Psates and Maria having reached the end of their lives."
P.Paris 20, parties to the inheritance division.
P.Paris 20: "all from this city of Panopolis."
P.Paris 20: Pachymios described as *porphyropōlēs*; Ioannes as "himself also a purple-seller."
P.Paris 20: "Aurelia Tarsene, another full sister of theirs, a widow."
P.Paris 20, invocation: "In the name of the Lord and Master Jesus Christ, our God and Saviour."
The notary of P.Paris 21bis (592 CE) was Ioulios; a notary named Dioskoros appears in documents from This around 600 CE; the later documents (605-616 CE) name a notary called Paulos.

SB 1.4504 (corpus id 6312, 613 CE) and P.Paris 21 (corpus id 6310, 616 CE) both open with Trinitarian invocations. The evolution from Christological to Trinitarian opening formulas in Egyptian documentary practice during this period may reflect post-Chalcedonian theological developments in the Egyptian church.
P.Paris 20, inheritance division clause.
P.Paris 20: *tēs elachistēs hypostaseōs* -- "the small estate." The diminutive is standard but not meaningless; it signals modest means.
P.Paris 20: Ioannes and Tarsene acknowledge they have "no claim against their most beloved other brother Pachoumis."
P.Paris 20, ratification of the 592 sale.
P.Paris 20, penalty clause: "If they should challenge him about this, they shall pay as penalty twelve gold nomismata."
P.Paris 20, common property clause.

The legal status of the two Charistons is ambiguous. The term *autokinēta* ("self-moving") in the enumeration of property types was the standard late Roman legal category encompassing livestock and, in some contexts, unfree laborers. Whether these men were enslaved, bonded, or contractually obligated craftsmen who owed service to the estate cannot be determined from the document alone.

On the category of *autokinēta* in late Roman property law, see the Codex Justinianus and the Egyptian documentary tradition of inheritance divisions, e.g., P.Cair.Masp. 3.67313 (corpus id 15584, 6th century), which divides an estate including "two persons who are siblings."

On the cult of Apa Kollouthos, see the hagiographical traditions of Upper Egypt. Kollouthos was venerated as a physician-saint and martyr of the Diocletianic persecution, with his cult center in the Theban region.
SB 1.4503 (corpus id 6311), dated 10 January 605 CE (Tybi 15, Year 3 of Phocas, 10th indiction).
SB 1.4503: Dioskoros described as *misthios porphyropōlēs*, contracting "to serve in the craft alongside your other hired workers for a period of two years."
SB 1.4503: "I receive from you as wages for the full two years: nineteen artabai of grain."
On the value of wheat in late antique Egypt, one artaba was roughly equivalent to 1/3-1/2 nomisma in the late 6th-early 7th century, though prices fluctuated with harvest conditions.

On the dual-currency economy of late antique Egypt, Egypt paid 2.6 million solidi per year to Constantinople, but local wages and small transactions were frequently conducted in kind (grain, wine, oil).
SB 1.4503, employee penalty clause: "I shall pay you as penalty one gold nomisma."
SB 1.4503, employer penalty clause: "you must pay me my full wages."
SB 1.4503: "Aurelius Dioskoros son of Harsynios, hired purple-worker, and his mother Tibellas."
SB 1.4503, dating formula: "In the reign of our most serene lord Flavius Phocas, the eternal Augustus and Imperator, year three."
On the fall of Maurice and the accession of Phocas in November 602, see Theophylact Simocatta, *History* 8.7-12. Maurice and his six sons were executed at Chalcedon.
SB 1.4505 (corpus id 6313), dated 11 October 606 CE (Phaophi 14, Year 4 of Phocas, 12th indiction).
SB 1.4505, identification of borrower and terms.
SB 1.4505, repayment terms: "seventy-five koloba of unadulterated new wine, of seven xestai each, with jars, at the vineyard of Lynochois."
A xestes was approximately 0.5 liters; 7 xestai per kolobon yields roughly 3.5 liters; 75 koloba therefore equals approximately 262 liters.
SB 1.4505: "at the time of the vintage, God willing, thirteenth indiction."
SB 1.4505, witness: "Ioannes son of Abraamios, priest from This."
SB 1.4504 (corpus id 6312), dated 18 November 613 CE (Hathyr 22, Year 4 of Heraclius, 2nd indiction).
SB 1.4504, loan and repayment terms.
The consistent ratio of roughly 75 koloba per nomisma between 606 and 613 suggests price stability in the local wine market across this period.
SB 1.4504, dating formula.

On the fall of Phocas and the accession of Heraclius in October 610, see Theophanes, *Chronographia* AM 6102. The famous exchange between Heraclius and Phocas is recorded in several Byzantine sources, though its historicity is uncertain.
On Egypt's fiscal contribution to Constantinople, see Justinian's *Edict 13* and the analysis in R.S. Bagnall, *Egypt in Late Antiquity* (Princeton, 1993).
On Maurice's reign (582-602), see the comprehensive treatment in M. Whitby, *The Emperor Maurice and His Historian* (Oxford, 1988).
The *Strategikon* attributed to Maurice is edited and translated by G.T. Dennis, *Maurice's Strategikon: Handbook of Byzantine Military Strategy* (Philadelphia, 1984).
On the mutiny of 602, Theophylact Simocatta provides the primary account.

P.Paris 21bis and the wine loans both refer to the weight standard of "phylloph" (*phyllōn*, "of the leaves"), a local or regional standard whose precise origins and definition remain debated in papyrological scholarship.
P.Paris 21bis, witness subscriptions.
P.Paris 21 (corpus id 6310, 616 CE), witnesses include Elias son of Besatas (priest) and Mousaios son of Papnouthios (priest).
P.Paris 21bis: "I wrote on their behalf at their request, Psates son of Papnouthios, deacon, from This."
P.Paris 20: "on the lane of the holy place of Apa Kollouthos."
On Apa Kollouthos, see the Coptic hagiographical corpus and the archaeological evidence for his cult in the Panopolite and Thinite nomes.

The White Monastery (Deir el-Abyad), founded by Pgol and expanded by Shenoute of Atripe (c. 348-465 CE), was one of the largest monastic complexes in late antique Egypt, located near Sohag, close to Panopolis.
P.Cair.Masp. 3.67312 (corpus id 15098, 31 March 567 CE), will of Flavius Theodoros from Antinoopolis, naming the monastery of "Apa Senouthes in the Panopolite nome" as heir.
On the evolution of documentary invocations, see R.S. Bagnall, "Religious Conversion and Onomastic Change in Early Byzantine Egypt," *Bulletin of the American Society of Papyrologists* 19 (1982).
P.Paris 21 (corpus id 6310), dated 3 June 616 CE (Payni 9, Year 6 of Heraclius, 5th indiction).
P.Paris 21, sellers: "Aureliae Pyras, daughter of Besatas, whose mother is Maria, acting also on behalf of her sister Rose; and Antheria, daughter of Senouthes, whose mother is Kyra."
P.Paris 21: "a complete house with all its rights, situated in the same village of This, on the lane called that of the phylake."
P.Paris 21: "Price: thirteen gold keratia."
P.Paris 21, eastern boundary: "the house of Pebos son of Horos and the house of the purchaser Pachymios."

P.Paris 21, witnesses. The possibility that Elias son of Besatas is the son of the 592 witness Besatas son of Elias, and that Mousaios son of Papnouthios is related to the 592 scribe Psates son of Papnouthios, rests on onomastic probability rather than certainty.
P.Paris 21: "from Panopolis, now by the providence of God residing in the same village of This" (*nyn de pronoia theou enoikounti*).
On the Arab conquest of Egypt, completed in 642 CE, see A.J. Butler, *The Arab Conquest of Egypt and the Last Thirty Years of the Roman Dominion* (Oxford, 1902; 2nd ed. 1978).
The Sasanian Persian occupation of Egypt lasted from 619 to 629 CE. Heraclius's reconquest and the subsequent Arab invasion occurred within Pachymios's plausible lifetime.

On continuity in documentary practice between the Byzantine and early Arab periods in Egypt, see P.M. Sijpesteijn, *Shaping a Muslim State: The World of a Mid-Eighth-Century Egyptian Official* (Oxford, 2013).

sources.translation: [...] in the reign and consulship of our most divine lord of the inhabited world, Flavius Mauricius, the eternal Augustus and Imperator, year ten, Payni 20, beginning of the 11th indiction, in the village of This in the Theban province. The Aurelii Psates son of Abraamios, whose mother is Tmouthes, purple-seller, and his wife Maria, daughter of Philamon, whose mother is Taniskene, all from the city of Panopolis, but now for a long time resident in the village of This of the Thinite nome, providing on their behalf a signatory and witnesses of this perpetual sale, those who sign below, we set forth this deed of sale as a simple written guarantee, to abide by this lawful sale and not to violate it in perpetuity; to Aurelius Pachymios, our most sweet son, himself also a purple-seller, from the same village, greetings. By which we acknowledge of our own free will and voluntary decision and guileless conscience and unwavering judgment and unrepentant choice, with firm and right mind, with the fullest right of ownership and with every authority for perpetual possession, that we have sold and conveyed in good faith to you today, having been persuaded, the property belonging to us: a dilapidated house and a cell together with the bare land, with all their rights, situated in the same village on the lane called that of the heirs of Pachot and the oil-press of the heirs of Pachareabe, having come to us by purchase from Viktor son of Psaios and Senouthes son of Phaliout, in accordance with the force of the two old sale documents which are also deposited with you by us. The boundaries on all sides of the aforesaid house and bare land and cell are as follows: to the south, the oil-press of the heirs of Pachareabe; to the north, adjoining the house of Pebos son of Horos; to the west, the house of the heirs of Viktor the priest and of Pebos son of Horos, into which the door opens; to the east, the public lane — or whoever the neighbors may be on all sides, from the ground to the sky. And so that you, the purchaser Pachymis, may own and control these and have full ownership, and improve and build, and sell and resell if you wish, and bequeath to heirs and successors and assigns, and donate and make use of them in whatever manner you wish for all time in perpetuity. The price mutually agreed upon and approved, upon the assessment of Senouthes the builder from the same village: three imperial simple tested gold nomismata less one keration, by the weight-standard of Phylloph(?), total: 3 gold nomismata less 1. Which complete price we, the sellers Psates and Maria his wife, have received from you the purchaser Pachymios, by hand-to-hand payment from the house, in full count, by which we have been persuaded. The guarantee of these things being provided by us and by our heirs and successors and assigns, with every guarantee, against everyone who may make a claim, whether on account of our name or public obligation or private debt or anything else whatsoever at any time, of necessity. And if it should happen — which may it not — that any eviction or dispute should arise concerning these matters, then we the sellers and our heirs and successors and assigns at our own expense shall ward off and repel all claimants or challengers, or else to repay the aforesaid price in double together with all expenses and costs spent on the improvement of the buildings; and for any legal proceedings, if they should arise, to restore the like in double to you, and further to pay as a penalty two simple gold pieces, while this sale remains unbreakable and unshakeable in perpetuity, which we have set forth for security, being valid and firm wherever it may be produced. And having been asked the formal question, we so agreed. We, the Aurelii Psates and Maria his wife, the aforementioned, have made this sale willingly and having been persuaded, and all the written terms are acceptable to us, as stated above. I wrote on their behalf at their request, Psates son of Papnouthios, deacon, from This. I, Besatas son of Elias, deacon, from This, witness the sale, having heard from those who made it. I, Athanasios son of Paamios, priest, from This, witness the sale, having heard from those who made it. Written through me, Ioulios, notary.

sources.translation:
In the name of the holy and life-giving and consubstantial Trinity, Father and Son and Holy Spirit. Written in the month of Hathyr 3, of the fourth indiction.

Aurelia Eudoxia, daughter of Georgios, whose mother is Ruth [...], with the consent and agreement of her husband Anoufios son of Isakios, taxpayer (syntelestes), from the village (kome) of Aphrodite of the Antaiopolite nome, to Aurelios Menas, son of the most blessed Christophoros, originating from the same village of Aphrodite. Greetings.

I acknowledge, the aforementioned Eudoxia daughter of Georgios, and with the consent of her husband Anoufios son of Isakios, taxpayer (syntelestes), through this my written guarantee (asphaleia), that is, sale (prasis), by voluntary decision, self-chosen will, irrevocable and unchangeable and unalterable judgment, with purest intention and clean conscience and guileless purpose and upright mind, together also swearing by the Almighty God and the victory of the imperial salvation:

that I have sold to you, the aforementioned Menas son of the most blessed Christophoros the merchant (pragmateutes), and have registered to you and have ceded and have relinquished to you, from now for all subsequent and perpetual time, with fullest right of ownership and in good faith and with all authority and for eternal proprietary possession, my property which came to me by lawful inheritance from my most blessed father: my whole share of the old, dilapidated house called (the house) of Ionathan the builder, situated in the northern part of the said village of Aphrodite, on the street of the holy catholic church, opening to the west onto the public street, with every indefeasible right, from foundation to the sky above, once and for all simply, as I had possession while my most blessed father was still alive, leaving nothing of my aforementioned whole share of the aforesaid old, dilapidated house, and from whatever right, from foundation to the sky above, as stated above.

The neighbors of the aforesaid old house on the four sides are these: to the south, a pillar (pessos) of the house of Menas Chichoites the coppersmith (chalkopratos); to the east, the great farmstead (epaulis) of the heirs of Promauos; to the north, the house of the heirs of Petros; or whoever the neighbors may be on all sides.

At a price mutually agreed upon and satisfactory, complete and fair, of two-thirds of a gold imperial approved nomisma in exchange, at the weight standard of two at the standard of the same village.

Total: 2/3 of a gold nomisma in exchange, 2 1/12(?) keratia. Which price in gold I have received, the vendor Eudoxia daughter of Georgios, and with the consent of her husband Anoufios son of Isakios, from you the purchaser Menas son of Christophoros, by your hand into my hands, in the sight of the lawful witnesses subscribing below.

So that from now in perpetuity, full in number and weight, together with the sale having been made, at which price and being satisfied, henceforth you the same purchaser Menas son of Christophoros shall hold and be master of and have dominion over my aforementioned whole share of the aforesaid dilapidated old house called (the house) of Ionathan the builder, and from whatever right, from foundation to the sky above, according to the above specification, and to dwell in and manage and administer and build or build upon and lease and sublease and transmit to your own heirs and successors and holders, and to use and enjoy in whatever manner of ownership seems good to you, without hindrance and without impediment, with no one challenging or being about to challenge, neither me nor my heirs or co-heirs or those related to me by birth or next of kin.

And the one who challenges at any time I shall necessarily repel. But if I am negligent in the confirmation (bebaiosis), I shall repay the price threefold, together with the applicable expenses and costs, provided that this sale shall be valid and binding everywhere it is presented, which having been written in a single copy, I willingly and satisfied have placed before you, without force or deceit or compulsion or any fraud or fear or any guile, in the public archive and completed according to the laws, with the subscription of the one who subscribes on my behalf, and the customary witnesses who at my request have witnessed.

And I have issued it to you for security and to all things written therein and for the confirmation and vindication of this sale, with all my existing and future property, movable and immovable and self-moving, generally and specifically, in every kind and type, by way of pledge and right of hypothek, as if by judicial decision.

And being formally asked whether all things written are so, and that I will give and do and observe them, I have acknowledged.

Aurelia Eudoxia, daughter of Georgios, whose mother is Ruth, with the consent of her husband Anoufios son of Isakios: the aforesaid, I have made this sale and I have received the aforesaid full price of two-thirds of a gold nomisma, and I shall confirm it with every confirmation in every respect, and all things written therein are satisfactory to me, and I have sworn the divine oath as aforesaid. Aurelios Ioannes (son of) Apollos, being requested, wrote on her behalf.

Aurelios Anoufios (son of) Isakios, the aforementioned: I have made this sale, and I hold the aforesaid full price of two-thirds of a gold nomisma, and we shall confirm with every confirmation in every respect, and all things written therein are satisfactory to us, and I have sworn the divine oath as aforesaid.

Aurelios Ioannes (son of) Hermauos: I witness this sale, having heard from the maker; I also witness the payment of the gold, two-thirds of a nomisma as the price.

Aurelios Daueit (son of) Phoibammon: I witness this sale, having heard from the makers; I also witness the payment of the gold, two-thirds of a nomisma as the price.

Aurelios Komasios (son of) Victor: I witness this sale, having heard from the makers; I also witness the payment of the gold, two-thirds of a nomisma as the price.

Through me, Apa Rasios the lawyer (nomikos), it was written and I authenticate it.

sources.translation:
…
                (1. Hd.) dort, damit sie nicht faul sind. Einen Tremissis an Rotbraunem und einen an Krimnonrot und einen an Purpurrot
                      bitte ich zu kaufen und mir zu schicken. Mach aber eine Artabe an Kichererbsen für Pharaklas und schicke sie mir,
                      und kaufe zwei Solidi an schönen, perfekten Pergamenten und schicke sie mir.
               Wenn ich also wegen des Herrn die gemeinsamen Kinder etwas ermüdet habe und sie schrieben, … und werde ich tadeln, damit ich sie nicht wieder ermüde und
                      sie schreiben müssen.  Ich habe aber erfahren, daß Theodote in die Gewalt eines Dämons geraten ist, und ich habe (deswegen) ein wenig vom Gewand des
                      Propheten Abba Schenute genommen und ihr das durch Epimachos geschickt, damit Ihr es auf sie werft; und wenn sie nicht gesund wird,
                      (dann) schickt sie mir. Ich aber, Petros, schreibe meiner gottbefohlenen Herrin, daß die Große von denen des Abba Schenute
                      mich bat zu schreiben und Euch darum zu bitten, daß Ihr um Gottes willen dafür sorgt, daß für das Erstlingsopfer
             des seligen Apphu …, und Ihr die Eulogien, die Dir, Herrin, geschickt worden waren,
                      sofort durch Iohannes, den Kameltreiber, sendet, der … Solidi ausgezahlt hat (?) … von uns
                      dort, und mehr gibt es nicht.  Und sie bittet mir wieder Antworten darauf zu schreiben, wozu ich Euch schrieb,
                      und schreibt nicht wieder anderes über andere Dinge. (2. Hd.) Ich schreibe anderes, und nehme auch von anderen Dingen etwas auf!
                      Liste der Euch von unserer Herrin, der Vorsteherin, geschickten Dinge: für das Konto des Herrn: 1 Paar Laken;
             für den Herrn Iohannes und den Herrn Georgios 2 kleine Sonnensegel (?);  für die Herren mitsamt der gottbefohlenen Theodosia
                      3 kleine Kisten.
Translation: A. Jördens, P.Louvre 3 234

… since … and after they had completed their lives … to have left undividedly their entire property in the field of our 
village of Arabon and in Antinoopolis, and part of a house situated in the same city of Antinoopolis, from the movable, immovable things and live-stock, and … we have divided 
between us the sellable things of our said blessed parents, from valuable to cheap objects and worth of an assarium, except for the things which are in the field … 
from different … people … into our hand … both of … them to be sold by us in any way we can … the price of them or to divide these things between us in equal share by just division. 
And now, after these, as there was doubt between us both concerning the said things in the field of our same blessed parents, since the property is taxable to the fund of our village … 
the synteleia of which and tax collection by the Public Treasury we could not bear to endure and we have often wished to give it away to the poor, and turn away from it, 
but also the rest of the estate on the pretext of the same property, until when, through the mediation of some friends, among whom … and the clarissimus uncle on our mother’s side, 
lord Kollouthos the scriniarius, we assumed their management over again and have made a just division of them for each other and satisfying to our friends, 
as agreed between us both in the same lawful written deed of division, through which each of us acknowledges to have already been alloted the share that belongs to him, ordained as follows.
I, the aforementioned Philemon … acknowledge, through this lawful deed of division of ours, that I have been alloted as my inheritance, 
without transgression or repentance in any way or at any time, 
(a) the the estate called Apalasias, situated on the Nile waters of Papnomthis, right opposite the monastery of Ama Tapollotos 
of most sacred memory, on the plain of the village of Arabon, with all its rights and all the appurtenances and dwelling and tower and reservoir and various trees, 
fruit-bearing and not fruit-bearing, and palms, and its entire area, both interior and exterior, within its old boundaries … of twenty four arourae more or less of plain land, 
together with a vine-bearing (piece of) land, and also the additional donation of the land flooded by the Nile that is there and its banks that have formed, that is shores, 
God willing, for every chance and circumstance they will be seen at each time, together with the dependencies of the same property, and the seven cucumber-beds with it, 
currently fruit-bearing, so that I, together with those who will succeed me and will receive all my inheritance, by just aim and division, 
according to the considered agreements to hold, rule, and own continually, from now and forever.
I have also been allotted together with these, and no less, (b) an entire house situated in the same village of Arabon, 
near the holy church of the passing-through-all-trials martyr Apa Theodoros, open to the east, together with its original door and its side entrance, with all its rights, 
and the cottage attached to this house and all that it contains and two artificial wells, in general with every contracted for and delivered house-rent that may occur from them, 
so that I, the aforewritten Philemon be allowed, together with my heirs, successors and possessors after me to hold continually and perpetually with the right of ownership, 
without hindrance or let, necessarily in general.
And in addition to these I have similarly been allotted (c) two persons who are siblings, the male one called Besas, and the female one called Matrona, 
together with their future descendants, of the … peculium for them each year and sent to me … Translation: Law in Social Networks Project (Warsaw)

⳩ In the second year of reign and consulate of our most divine lord Flavius Iustinus, eternal Augustus  and Emperor, on the fifth (day) of Pharmouthi of the fifteenth indiction, in clarissima  Antinoopolis. 
⳩ I, Flavius Theodoros, exceptor of the ducal office of the Thebaid, son of Phoibammon of blessed memory, former scholasticus of the court of the Thebaid, hailing from the  city of the Antinoites, 
make this written will in accordance with what follows … being distressed and fearing about the future, lest I die against the expectations before I compose my will and make dispositions concerning my property;
for this reason, being sane and in my right mind, my reasoning being accurate, and, God willing, having sound wits and all senses, and keeping my reasoning unaffected, 
I have made this will in the presence of seven witnesses summoned on my request, who gathered for this reason, being Roman citizens, adults of good reputation, 
who signed this will of mine below and sealed it at one place, time, and hour, with no other action interrupting it, according to the force of the law. 
I have dictated this will in Greek words and I commanded it to be put in writing, and I want it to have its own power and warranty in all its paragraphs, 
and (I want) it not to have inferior position and power than a codicil and any last will and fideicommissary letter, if brought and produced in front of every magistrate and authority, 
in every time and on any occasion, according  to the imperial requirements given by laws. If it seems right to me to make a codicil or codicils after this and to make principal dispositions in them, 
I want and order that this codicil made by me, or these codicils made by me, have and will have the same power and legal validity, as confirmed in this my will.
May I live longer and manage my usual business, if God the Saviour commands, but since death is unavoidable and one must depart from the human things, 
I appoint and want and order the dikaion of the holy monastery called (the monastery of) Apa Senouthes located on the hill Triphion in the Panopolite nome, 
(represented) through the most God-loving archimandrite Petros, and the dikaion of the monastery called  (the monastery of) Apa Mousaios located on the hill A..l... in the Hermopolite nome, 
(represented) through Phoibammon, its most God-loving hegumen; and Herais, my most noble grandmother on my mother’s  side, to be my heirs to all things left by me at the moment of death, 
and legal actions, obligations, and  legal matter, from the precious down to the smallest thing up to a penny, according to the following command and disposition made by me for each of the said heirs. 
All the others who belong to my kin  or who believe to belong to it according to the rights of inheritance shall be disinherited, not because of any outrage – Heaven forbid – but because I deemed it right, 
having used my own sound and accurate reasoning. 
I want and order that Petros, the most pious archimandrite, that is the dikaion of the  same holy monastery of Apa Senouthes, 
have as their inheritance all immovable goods left by me at the  moment of my death in the Hermopolite, and Antinoite, and Panopolite nomes, and in other likely places, 
and to have not less as their inheritance, and all my immovable goods situated in Antinoopolis and in Hermopolis, on the condition that all the collected rents and incomes from them are to be spent  on pious distributions. 
I want and order the same most pious apa Petros, that is the dikaion of the same holy monastery of Apa Senouthos, to sell after my death my family house located in Antinoopolis, 
which I inherited from my father, with all the rights, and stable, and buildings belonging to this house; and to spend the price on ransoming captives and other pious distributions.
I also want this most pious man, that is the dikaion of the same holy monastery, to sell everything acquired from me and my substance, which came to me thanks to the piety of my wife of blessed memory, 
and to spend the price of sale for the holy prosphora for my wife of blessed memory or other pious distributions, for the remittal of her sins and above-mentioned … . 
I want and order Phoibammon, the most pious prior of those from Apa Mousaios, that is the dikaion of this monastery, to have as their own inheritance all movable goods left by me at the moment of my death, 
and to spend their revenues on distributions which his God belovedness considers right. 
For I am convinced that this God-fearing man will give … in its entirety without diminishing, for the remittal of my sins.
And I want and consider it right that my above-named most noble grandmother on  my mother’s side shall have by the title of succession a plot of land named [blank] under [blank] situated in [blank] with everything 
that justly belongs to it and in all its entity, and I want it to satisfy her, so that she shall not demand anything else under the lex Falcidia vis-a-vis the dikaion of none of the two aforesaid monasteries 
or the aforementioned Petros and Phoibammon, the most pious priors of  the said two monasteries and my heirs, since the things left to them in this my will are to be expended  on pious distributions. 
I want and order that all my slave men and women (enslaved) for any chance or reason will be freed at the moment of my death, with their peculia; and Petros and Phoibammon, my most pious heirs, 
shall give once six gold coins of good weight to each. 
I want and order my nurse Tadelphe and her daughter Leontia to have every year undivided twelve gold coins of good weight from my aforementioned most pious heirs Petros and Phoibammon that is dikaia of holy monasteries; 
and I want Petros and Phoibammon, my most pious heirs, to give to Re.a…, nurse of my mother Lia of most blessed memory, to be given in addition the bequest given in her (my mother’s) will …
